title: Defining and Using Translation Tables
text: Defining and Using Translation Tables Uniface uses device translation tables to handle output to screens and printers, and keyboard translation tables to handle input from keyboards. For every system supported, Uniface supplies a set of translation tables in the USYS library. These default tables are sufficient in most cases. Define your own device translation table if you want to modify how a device works, or address a device that is not yet supported. For example, you may want to use national character sets or color preferences. If a device does not support a certain feature, such as graphic borderlines or color, the device can find a suitable approximation or leave the feature out. Tip: If you need to define a translation table, it is easier to adapt an existing table than to create a new one from scratch. An existing definition can match your particular device quite closely, if not exactly. Related Links Device Translation Table Syntax A device translation table contains delimiters that mark the beginning and end of the table and sections within the table. Each section contains mapping definitions that define an output code sequence or character, the corresponding Uniface meta code (a function mnemonic or meta character), and optional description or comments. Keyboard Translation Table Syntax Keyboard translation tables map input codes from keyboards or database to logical Uniface functions and characters. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
